When I was young, you had a village, and if someone said something untrue, people knew them. You could look them in the eye, and you knew if their words held weight or if they were just blowing smoke. Now, with this internet and these 'social media' things, it is like a vast, noisy piazza where every beggar and every wise man is shouting at once, and you never know who is telling the truth. I spend many hours looking at these screens, trying to keep up with the world, but it is exhausting because the lies travel faster than the truth, always. For me, the way to manage this noise is to slow down, to be very careful, just like when I am walking for my run and I must watch where I step. I do not trust the headline; I do not trust the picture alone. If something makes my heart race, if it seems too shocking or too perfect, I stop. I do not share it right away. I think, 'Where is the source for this?' If it is just some random name with a funny profile picture, I treat it like gossip from a stranger—I listen, but I do not believe it until I have seen it confirmed by someone I know, someone solid. It is like looking at a film; you need the director, not just a random clip someone cuts out. I try to remember the lessons of my own life, the things my family taught me, that a good story has roots, and a lie, even a clever one, usually floats away quickly when you look closely enough. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: This whole business of what is true and what is not online, it gives me a headache, truly. When I was young, you had a village, and if someone said something untrue, people knew them. You could look them in the eye, and you knew if their words held weight or if they were just blowing smoke. Now, with this internet and these 'social media' things, it is like a vast, noisy piazza where every beggar and every wise man is shouting at once, and you never know who is telling the truth. I spend many hours looking at these screens, trying to keep up with the world, but it is exhausting because the lies travel faster than the truth, always. For me, the way to manage this noise is to slow down, to be very careful, just like when I am walking for my run and I must watch where I step. I do not trust the headline; I do not trust the picture alone. If something makes my heart race, if it seems too shocking or too perfect, I stop. I do not share it right away. I think, 'Where is the source for this?' If it is just some random name with a funny profile picture, I treat it like gossip from a stranger—I listen, but I do not believe it until I have seen it confirmed by someone I know, someone solid. It is like looking at a film; you need the director, not just a random clip someone cuts out. I try to remember the lessons of my own life, the things my family taught me, that a good story has roots, and a lie, even a clever one, usually floats away quickly when you look closely enough. It takes a great deal of patience, which I must force myself to have, because this constant barrage of bad news can make one quite anxious, making one feel unsettled in their own home.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 84-year-old immigrant man with low formal education, high conscientiousness, and high neuroticism, spending several hours daily on social media. His tone should be cautious, reflective, slightly anxious, and grounded in simple, tangible experiences (like village life, running, cinema). The advice must be practical for someone unfamiliar with modern digital literacy but accustomed to traditional methods of vetting information. The response draws parallels between the chaotic online world and familiar concepts (noisy piazza, watching one's step).
